_meta.description: Strategies 23–27 with long definitions added (v1.3). This is chunk 05 of the full set.

speaking: Students will orally {function} {content_focus} using {supports} (e.g., {frames}), producing {product} that meets {criteria}.
Students will discuss {content_focus} in pairs, using {frames} to {function} and incorporate {target_vocab}.
listening: Students will comprehend {input_type} on {content_focus} and demonstrate understanding by {response_mode} using {supports}.
Students will follow {procedural_input} and {function} (e.g., sequence, identify) the steps for {content_focus}.
reading: Students will read {text_type} about {content_focus} and {function} (e.g., identify, infer) key ideas using {strategy} and {target_vocab}.
Students will use {strategy} (e.g., cognates, text features) to make meaning from {text_type} about {content_focus}.
writing: Students will write a {genre} to {purpose} about {content_focus}, using {frames}/{target_vocab} and {language_features}.
Students will revise a short paragraph to {function} (e.g., explain, justify) understanding of {content_focus} using {criteria}.

speaking+listening: In structured talk, students will {function} about {content_focus}, using {frames} while peers listen and {response_mode}.
reading+writing: Students will read {text_type} on {content_focus} and write a {genre} that {purpose}, using {target_vocab} and {criteria}.
all: Through {framework}, students will listen to {input_type}, discuss {content_focus}, read {text_type}, and write a {genre} that {purpose}, with {supports}.

strategies.core_principle: Brief, purposeful switches to L1 to unlock comprehension, then immediate return to English.
implementation_steps: Clarify in L1 in a sentence or two; immediately restate in English.
Monitor frequency and reduce as proficiency grows.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Planned, purposeful shifts between L1 and English to clarify meaning, build connections, and maintain flow of instruction.
definitions.long_en: Strategic Code-Switching is the intentional use of students’ first language alongside English during instruction to enhance comprehension and learning. Teachers may briefly switch to L1 to clarify a complex idea, emphasize a key point, or check understanding, then immediately return to English. This differs from unplanned translation: it is purposeful, targeted, and time-limited. Strategic code-switching validates students’ linguistic resources while keeping English as the main medium of instruction. For example, a teacher may explain a metaphor in Portuguese before guiding students to re-express it in English. When used judiciously, it lowers anxiety, accelerates transfer, and deepens conceptual understanding. Overuse, however, risks reducing exposure to English and encouraging dependence. The goal is to leverage L1 as a scaffold that bridges to independent English use, not as a replacement for English instruction.
look_fors: Brief, purposeful switches to L1 with clear return to English
Clarification of complex ideas or cultural references in L1
Students re-expressing ideas in English after L1 support
Teacher signaling intentionality of the switch
non_examples: Unplanned shifts to L1 whenever students struggle
Overreliance on L1 with little English practice

strategies.core_principle: Explicitly map learned content/vocabulary from L1 to English (and vice versa) to ensure transfer.
implementation_steps: List key L1 concepts; co-create English equivalents in two-column charts.
Compare cognates/false cognates; apply via English summaries/presentations.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A structured lesson phase where teachers explicitly connect learning in L1 to English, reinforcing transfer of concepts and language.
definitions.long_en: The Bridge is a component of biliteracy instruction where the teacher deliberately connects concepts learned in students’ L1 to English, highlighting similarities and differences in vocabulary, grammar, and discourse. Typically taught after a unit in L1, the Bridge involves side-by-side comparisons, co-created charts, and discussion of cognates, morphology, and syntax. Students explore how knowledge transfers across languages and practice applying it in English tasks. The Bridge validates L1 learning, strengthens cross-linguistic awareness, and makes transfer visible. It is most powerful when embedded in a systematic biliteracy framework, ensuring that both languages are valued. The Bridge is not a quick translation exercise; it is a reflective process that empowers students to see themselves as biliterate and to apply skills flexibly. Avoid treating it as an afterthought; intentional planning is essential for its impact.
look_fors: Side-by-side charts of L1 and English terms
Discussion of cognates, morphology, and syntax
Students applying knowledge in English after L1 learning
non_examples: Literal translation of texts without reflection
Focusing only on English without validating L1 learning

strategies.core_principle: Invite students’ full linguistic repertoire for meaning-making and expression.
applicable_contexts: Brainstorming/drafting in L1 before English output
implementation_steps: Offer multilingual resources; allow L1 brainstorming/notes; peer translation tasks.
Bridge to English products with scaffolds and modeling.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Pedagogy that allows learners to flexibly use their full linguistic repertoires (L1 and English) to learn and express meaning.
definitions.long_en: Translanguaging is a teaching approach that legitimizes and strategically incorporates students’ full linguistic repertoires into classroom learning. It allows learners to use both their L1 and English fluidly for meaning-making, collaboration, and identity expression. In practice, teachers may encourage brainstorming or drafting in L1, peer discussions in mixed languages, or bilingual word walls and resources. Students then bridge back to English for formal outputs, such as essays or presentations. This approach deepens comprehension, affirms cultural identity, and develops metalinguistic awareness. Translanguaging is not the same as random code-switching; it is purposeful, designed with clear objectives, and linked to outcomes in English. Overuse without bridging risks limiting English exposure; the key is balance and intentionality. When implemented thoughtfully, translanguaging transforms bilingualism from a challenge into a resource for learning.
look_fors: Students using L1 resources to support English tasks
Bilingual displays and word walls in classroom
Teacher prompts bridging L1 work to English output
non_examples: Teacher translating everything directly into L1
Students using only L1 without bridging to English
No clear objective guiding multilingual use
